data.title: USDA attache projects Australia 2026/27 wheat crop at 29 million T
data.description:
A report released on Tuesday by the U.S. Department of Agriculture's attache in Australia projected the country's 2026/27 wheat harvest at 29 million metric tons, down 19% from the prior year.

  • The report, issued by the USDA's Foreign Agricultural Service post in Canberra, cited expectations of a smaller harvested area and a return to near-average wheat yields after relatively high yields in 2025/26.

  • "While the season has begun favorably in most key regions, uncertainty around in-season rainfall and the potential development of El Niño conditions present downside risks," the report said.

  • The report projected Australia's 2026/27 wheat exports at 23.5 million tons, down from 26 million in 2025/26, primarily due to lower production.

  • The report noted rising farm input costs due to geopolitical tensions in the Middle East. "Diesel and nitrogenous fertilizer prices have approximately doubled in the lead-up to planting," the report said.

  • However, the report said, "seasonal conditions remain the dominant factor influencing planting decisions and production outcomes."

  • Attache reports are not official USDA data. The USDA is scheduled to release updated official world crop production estimates for the 2025/26 marketing year, as well as its first official forecasts for 2026/27, in a monthly report on May 12.

  • To read the attache's full report, click: https://tinyurl.com/ypd6pnpz

data.title: Starbucks is seeing a resurgence, thanks to younger and lower-income customers
data.description:
By Claudia Assis

Coffee chain hikes outlook as sales top Wall Street's hopes

Starbucks' global same-store sales rose 6.2%, topping investors expectations for an increase of 4%.

Starbucks' plan to win more customers with ube macchiatos and other menu changes, faster service and a revamped loyalty program appears to be paying off.

The coffee chain late Tuesday reported a sharp rebound in sales for the quarter ended in March, leading it to raise its outlook for the year. The stock (SBUX) rose more than 5% in after-hours trading.

Starbucks said its global same-store sales rose 6.2%, whereas investors expected an increase of 4%. The company pinned that on a 3.8% increase in comparable transactions and a 2.3% rise in its average ticket.

U.S. same-store sales did even better - they rose 7.1%. And the company did well on revenue and per-share profit, beating Wall Street's expectations there too.

The quarter "marked the turn in our turnaround as our 'Back to Starbucks' plan drove both top- and bottom-line growth," CEO Brian Niccol said in a statement.

Starbucks guided for 2026 global and U.S. same-store sales growth of 5% "or greater," it said. That would be considerably more than Wall Street's expectations for 3.8% and would follow two years of same-store sales declines.

The company's fiscal second-quarter revenue rose 9% to $9.5 billion, which compares with expectations for $9.2 billion.

It reported a quarterly non-GAAP profit of 50 cents a share, compared with estimates for 43 cents a share, according to FactSet.

-Claudia Assis

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Storage stocks jump as Seagate's upbeat forecast fuels confidence in AI spending
data.description:
By Zaheer Kachwala

Shares of storage companies jumped in extended trading on Tuesday, after strong revenue and profit forecasts from Seagate Technology signaled that spending by enterprises on artificial intelligence equipment will remain strong.

Seagate's strong forecast and the late-day surge in storage stocks underscore investors' confidence that enterprise AI spending will sustain demand for data-storage equipment despite broader market concerns about the pace of AI adoption.

Companies have funneled increasing amounts of money into data drives, hard disks and other digital storage as they rushed to upgrade their artificial intelligence models and infrastructure.

Shares of Seagate soared 16% in extended trading, while Western Digital jumped 10%, Micron Technology climbed 3% and SanDisk rose 4%, adding a combined $60 billion to the four storage technology companies' market value.

"AI is amplifying demand across existing applications such as video, where large cloud providers are integrating AI in the platforms to boost user engagement and revenue opportunities, driving new video creation and the need to store it," said Seagate CEO Dave Mosley on a post-earnings conference call.

Seagate forecast fourth-quarter revenue of $3.45 billion, plus or minus $100 million, beating estimates of $3.16 billion, according to data compiled by LSEG.

Executives at Seagate and rival Western Digital have previously said their capacity has been fully allocated and sold out through calendar 2026.

In Tuesday's trading session, the Nasdaq fell almost 1%, hurt by concerns about the AI boom after a report said OpenAI has fallen short of its goals for new users and revenue in recent months.

Nasdaq futures rose 0.2% following Seagate's report, showing that traders expect tech stocks to partly rebound in Wednesday's session.

The rise in storage demand also comes alongside a surge in memory prices, as power-hungry data centers use a significant portion of high-bandwidth memory to store and process massive amounts of information.

The shortage of memory chips has left companies scrambling to find hardware that can aid their AI efforts and store data, benefiting companies such as Seagate that make physical hard drives.

Ahead of Tuesday's report, Seagate shares had already more than doubled, year to date.

data.title: Robinhood Earnings Fall Short. A Crypto Slump Is the Culprit. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Robinhood Markets reported first-quarter earnings that missed Wall Street's expectations because of a drop in cryptocurrency trading revenue.

The brokerage firm posted earnings per share of 38 cents on revenue of $1.07 billion. Analysts were looking for 39 cents and $1.14 billion, according to estimates compiled by FactSet. For the same period a year ago, earnings per share were 37 cents on revenue of $927 million.

Shares of Robinhood dropped 6.1% in after hours trading. A months-long slump in prices for Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies has weighed on Robinhood's stock, which is down 27% this year. The iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF is down 15%.

Under CEO Vlad Tenev, Robinhood has been striving to expand its sources of revenue and become a one-stop shop for customers' every financial need. It has launched financial advice and banking services for clients. And it is winning more customers for its Gold subscription service — the company said the number of subscribers hit a record 4.3 million, up 36% year over year. Robinhood Gold subscription revenue for the first quarter rose 32% to $50 million.

But the majority of Robinhood's revenue is transaction-based, and a slowdown in customer's trading activity can be a hindrance for the brokerage firm. Transaction-based revenue was $623 million for the first quarter, up 6% from the same period a year ago, but down 20% from the fourth quarter of 2025.

The company said cryptocurrencies revenue fell 47% year over year to $134 million.

Robinhood has also leaned into sports betting via its prediction markets offering, which enables customers to wager on the outcome of events such as football and basketball games. The company said other transaction revenue, which primarily consists of event contracts revenue, soared 320% to $147 million. The first quarter included the Super Bowl.

This is breaking news. Read a preview of Robinhood earnings below and check back for more analysis soon.

Robinhood Markets may be a digital brokerage firm, but to gauge its financial health, you really need to take the pulse of something else: crypto. That is because a significant portion of the Menlo Park, Calif.-based company's revenue comes from cryptocurrency trading.

Investors and analysts will closely watch Robinhood's first-quarter earnings report after the bell Tuesday to see just how much the recent rout in prices of cryptocurrencies such as Bitcoin and Ether has dampened the company's results.

Wall Street analysts anticipate that Robinhood will report earnings per share of 39 cents versus 37 cents for the same period a year ago, according to estimates compiled by FactSet. They expect revenue of $1.14 billion, compared with $927 million in the first quarter of last year.

Those expected improvements obscure an expected year-over-year drop in cryptocurrency trading revenue, from $252 million to $163.1 million. This figure was $221 million in the fourth quarter.

Those figures reflect the decline of crypto prices late last year, which has continued into 2026. The iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF is down 15% this year. Robinhood's stock has fallen 28%.

The company is also facing more competition for retail investors' crypto trades as other banks and brokerage firms launch their own cryptocurrency offerings. In early April, Charles Schwab announced that it will begin graduallying rolling out its cryptocurrency service to clients, enabling them to directly hold Bitcoin and Ether at the brokerage firm.

Under CEO Vlad Tenev, Robinhood has been striving to expand its sources of revenue and become a one-stop shop for customers' every financial need. For example, the company has launched financial advice and banking services for clients.

It has also leaned into sports betting via its prediction markets offering, which enables customers to wager on the outcome of events such as football and basketball games. Robinhood's first-quarter earnings report will likely show strong prediction market activity because the three-month period included the Super Bowl.

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Ribbon Communications Names Marmurek as CFO, to Succeed Townsend
data.description:
By Adam L. Cataldo

Ribbon Communications has picked Rick Marmurek to be its next chief financial officer, effective May 1.

Marmurek is expected to succeed current CFO John Townsend, who is leaving to take another professional opportunity, Ribbon said Tuesday. His departure is effective April 30.

Marmurek, who will also become chief accounting officer, has more than 35 years of experience in finance, Ribbon said. He has been with the company or its predecessors for more than 15 years, and has worked at Nokia and Coopers and Lybrand.

Based in Plano, Texas, Ribbon provides voice communications software, IP routing, and optical networking to mobile and wireline service providers.

Write to Adam L. Cataldo at adam.cataldo@wsj.com

data.title: Canadian Pacific Kansas City Raises Quarterly Dividend by 17.5%
data.description:
By Stephen Nakrosis

Canadian Pacific Kansas City said its board approved a 17.5% increase to its quarterly cash dividend.

The new dividend, of 0.268 Canadian dollars ($0.195), up from C$0.228, will be payable on July 27 to shareholders of record at the close of business on June 26, the railroad company said.

The new dividend, C$1.07 on an annual basis, represents a yield of 0.9%, based on the company's closing share price of C$118.64 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

In April of last year, the company raised its dividend 20%, from C$0.19 to C$0.228.

Write to Stephen Nakrosis at stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com

data.title: Timberland Bancorp Q2 EPS rises
data.description:
Overview

  • US regional bank's Q2 EPS rose 6% yr/yr to $0.90

  • Net income and net interest income both increased 6% from a year ago

  • Company repurchased 80,000 shares for $3.09 mln during the quarter

Outlook

  • Company did not provide specific guidance for future quarters or the full year

Result Drivers

  • CREDIT LOSS PROVISION - Higher provision for credit losses and increased non-accrual loans impacted results, primarily due to an isolated participation loan moved to non-accrual status

  • NET INTEREST INCOME - Year-over-year growth in net interest income supported results, though average interest-earning assets declined from the prior quarter

  • DEPOSIT GROWTH - Total deposits grew 2% from the prior quarter and 6% year-over-year, aiding funding stability

Company press release:
